MMA 616 · Individual Final

Priority Follow-Up: A Decision-Support Workspace for Alberta Child Care Licensing Inspections

A deterministic, governance-reviewed workspace that ranks licensed child care centres for inspector follow-up, built on Alberta Children's Services' public inspection data.
Course
MMA 616
Deliverable
Individual Final Project
Student
Zhanbo Yang
Date
July 15, 2026

2.Executive Summary

This project builds a decision-support workspace for one named user — Priya Desai, Regional Program Manager, Licensing & Monitoring, Alberta Children's Services — around one recurring decision:

Primary Decision

"Which licensed child care centres should inspectors follow up on first?"

The workspace turns Alberta's public child care licensing/inspection extract into a centre-level, ranked view that lets Priya prioritize inspector follow-up using only the compliance history the data actually records — no invented fields, scores, or predictions. It normalizes 15,772 raw inspection rows into a per-centre compliance history for 2,913 licensed centres, ranks them by a deterministic, governance-approved rule, surfaces observed follow-up gaps after enforcement, and generates a grounded, template-based explanation for why any selected centre appears where it does.

Main outcome: a working, real-data dashboard (dashboard/index.html) that implements 6 of 8 defined capabilities against the downloaded public-data extract, backed by a documented governance decision (Candidate B) selected after a two-stage evaluation that specifically checked for inspection-volume bias, same-visit citation inflation, and stale-history domination — and a full repository consistency audit confirming the workspace matches its own documentation with no blocking issues.

3.Workspace Design

CLAUDE.md — single source of truth

CLAUDE.md defines the project objective, scope (in/out), data grain and limitations, the 8-capability table, the approved production ranking, the actionable-centre rule, Human in the Loop boundaries, and an explicit "Must Never Produce" list. Every other document and the dashboard itself are required to stay consistent with it — the repository's final audit (Section 9) verified this directly.

Data pipeline

scripts/build_centre_summary.py is a single deterministic Python script implementing Capabilities 2–4 and 6: it loads the raw CSV, treats blank and literal "NULL" values as missing, removes exact duplicate rows, resolves the one Program ID recorded under two names, aggregates rows into one summary per centre, computes the observed follow-up-gap table, and produces three deterministic ranking candidates. It writes data/processed/centre-summary.json, follow-up-gaps.json, ranking-candidates.json, and pipeline-audit.json, plus a browser-loadable copy for the dashboard.

Capability map

capability-map.md enumerates exactly 8 capabilities, each justified against the dataset assessment and business discovery, each assigned one implementation form (Native action / Fixed pipeline / Connector / Subagent), with an explicit "Forms considered but rejected" section and a "Build First" rationale (Capability 4). See Section 6 for the full table.

Deterministic components

Implemented Capabilities 2–4, 6, and 7 are deterministic. Capability 1 is designed as a future connector for refreshed public-data extracts, and Capability 8 remains a future grounded Q&A Subagent. This was a deliberate governance choice — see Section 10.

Human-in-the-loop design

CLAUDE.md's Human in the Loop section fixes four decisions that remain Priya's (or her team's) alone: whether/when to dispatch an inspector, how to weigh the ranking against unrecorded context, whether an observed follow-up gap warrants action, and final judgment on any explanation the workspace provides. The workspace surfaces, ranks, and explains — it does not decide.

4.Data Source and Assessment

Source file
data/raw/child-care-open-data-202512.csv — Alberta Children's Services child care licensing/inspection open data extract.
Date range
Inspection dates 2024-07-02 through 2025-12-23 (~18 months) — despite the filename suggesting a single December 2025 snapshot, "202512" is the extract's pull date, not its coverage period.
Row grain
One row = one non-compliance citation tied to a specific inspection visit, identified by (Program ID, Inspection Date, Inspection Reason). A visit with no violation produces exactly one row with blank Non Compliance/Enforcement Action/Remedy Date.
Rows and centres
15,772 raw rows; 2,913 unique Program ID values (centres).

Data quality issues found

IssueDetail
Exact duplicate rows135 fully identical rows found and removed by the pipeline.
Literal "NULL" stringsUsed instead of a true blank in Remedy Date and some Inspection Date values; treated as missing throughout.
Missing inspection date39 rows have no usable Inspection Date and are excluded from all visit-based counts and ranking.
Dual program nameOne Program ID is recorded under two different Program Name values; identity is always resolved by Program ID.

Important limitations

  • No inspector identity or inspecting office/region field.
  • No severity rating — Enforcement Action is a fixed, ordered category, not a numeric score.
  • No open/closed case-status field — only whether a Remedy Date is present.
  • No enrollment/attendance data — only licensed Capacity.
  • No licence start date or unique visit identifier.
  • No indication of appeals or reversals of a violation or enforcement action.

5.Business Discovery

Priya Desai — Regional Program Manager, Licensing & Monitoring, Alberta Children's Services (Central Region). She oversees a team of field inspectors covering several hundred licensed programs, allocates inspector time weekly across routine renewals, complaint response, and enforcement follow-ups, and is accountable for timely follow-up on ORDER TO REMEDY and more serious actions.

Her recurring decision: "Given limited inspector time, which licensed child care centres should inspectors follow up on first?" — a repeating triage/allocation decision, not a one-time lookup.

Current pain

The raw data is a flat, 15,772-row, per-violation event log with no rollup and no severity tier. To know which of 2,913 centres deserve attention right now, someone would have to manually trace each centre's own row history — up to 56 rows for a single centre — checking for recurring violations, escalating enforcement, and whether a Remedy Date was ever logged after an action.

Before / after workflow

BeforeAfter
Supervisor reviews individual centre files reactively (e.g., after a new complaint), with no standing view of which centres are already trending toward repeat non-compliance. Supervisor opens a rolled-up view — citation counts, highest recorded Enforcement Action, recency, and reactive-reason mix per centre — and uses it to decide where to send inspector time next. This is a triage aid, not a case-management system.

Story #1 (recommended and implemented)

Decision → Pain → Change → Outcome

Decision: Priya needs to answer: "Which child care centres should inspectors follow up on first?"

Current pain: Answering this today means manually reviewing thousands of individual inspection records to piece together which centres show a pattern of repeated non-compliance and stronger enforcement actions over time.

What changes: Using only the recorded inspection history already in the data (Non Compliance, Enforcement Action, Inspection Date, Program ID/Program City), Priya can identify which centres show repeated non-compliance and stronger enforcement actions without manually reading every inspection record.

Business outcome: Priya can direct her team's limited inspection time to the centres showing repeated non-compliance and stronger enforcement actions first, instead of discovering those patterns only after another complaint or incident.

6.Capability Map

#CapabilityForm
1Ingest inspection recordsConnector
2Normalize recordsFixed pipeline
3Aggregate per-centre compliance historyFixed pipeline
4Rank centres by repeated non-compliance and enforcement actionsFixed pipeline
5Filter and segment the centre listNative action
6Detect follow-up gaps after enforcementFixed pipeline
7Explain why a centre is prioritizedFixed pipeline (deterministic template) — implemented
8Answer ad hoc questions about specific centres or the rankingSubagent — not yet implemented

Why deterministic methods were used for ranking (Capability 4)

The Enforcement Action field already contains a fixed vocabulary in which later actions represent a stronger regulatory response than earlier ones, and "repeated" is simply a count of citation rows per centre. Ordering by an existing categorical field and a count is fully deterministic — a Subagent was explicitly considered and rejected for this capability because it would make the ranking non-reproducible and harder to audit for no added benefit.

Why Capability 7 uses a deterministic template

The shipped explanation generator (buildCentreExplanation() in dashboard/index.html) produces its opening sentence, supporting facts, and closing limitation entirely from pre-approved aggregated fields — citation count, visits with a citation, highest recorded Enforcement Action, inspection/citation dates, and reactive-visit counts. It never reads or concatenates the free-text Non Compliance field, which is what a naive fixed-pipeline template would have struggled with (178 distinct texts vary too much in phrasing to concatenate cleanly). Because the explanation only needs structured facts, not free-text synthesis, a deterministic template is sufficient today. A Subagent remains a documented possible future enhancement if a richer, free-text-aware explanation is wanted later — Capability 8 remains that Subagent-form capability, not yet implemented.

7.Dashboard

dashboard/index.html follows a decision-first, one-screen structure: the answer to the primary decision sits first and most prominent; supporting panels either scope that answer, add a second observed signal, or explain/answer on demand rather than occupying standing screen space. This satisfies the "morning-coffee test" — Priya can see who to follow up on, and why, immediately.

Priority Follow-Up List

The primary panel. Ranks centres by the approved production rule (Section 9), defaults to the top 10 actionable centres, and provides View more / Show all / Back to top 10 controls so the decision-first default can be expanded deliberately rather than showing every centre at once.

Filters

A scope-filter control (City, Type of Program, service-type flags) sits inside the Priority list panel rather than as an independent panel, since it only narrows Priya's own coverage area — it does not change the ranking logic itself.

Follow-Up Gaps

A second panel surfaces centres with an enforcement action and a Remedy Date on record but no later inspection row — an observed absence in the record, explicitly captioned as not a claim that the underlying issue is open, closed, or resolved.

Why This Centre Is Prioritized

Appears only once a centre is selected from either table above. Generates a grounded, deterministic-template explanation (Capability 7) from that centre's own aggregated facts, and always closes with: "This explanation summarizes the recorded inspection history only. Final follow-up decisions remain with the program manager."

Q&A as a future capability

The "Ask About a Centre or the Ranking" panel is present in the layout but clearly and consistently labeled "not yet implemented" (panel tag, header subtitle, and audit banner) — Capability 8 is designed as a Subagent for open-ended questions but has not been built.

8.Evidence of Use

The pipeline has been run against the real dataset, producing the following recorded, verifiable outputs (data/processed/pipeline-audit.json):

StepRecorded outcome
NormalizationBlank and literal "NULL" values treated as missing across all fields.
Deduplication135 exact duplicate rows removed (15,772 → 15,637 rows).
Aggregation15,598 usable rows rolled up into 2,913 per-centre summaries (39 rows excluded for missing inspection date); 1 dual-named Program ID resolved.
Ranking comparisonThree deterministic candidates (A, B, C) computed and compared across top-10 and top-20 views — eval/ranking-candidate-comparison.md, eval/final-ranking-recommendation.md.
Production-ranking selectionCandidate B formally adopted — logs/ranking-governance-decision.md.
Dashboard renderingdashboard/index.html loads data/processed/dashboard-data.js and renders the real, production-ordered list, gaps table, and grounded explanations.
Final auditeval/final-project-audit.md — full repository consistency pass, all five sections PASS.

Concrete recorded output (pipeline audit)

{
  "raw_row_count": 15772,
  "exact_duplicate_rows_removed": 135,
  "rows_excluded_missing_inspection_date": 39,
  "unique_program_ids": 2913,
  "inspection_date_range": { "min": "2024-07-02", "max": "2025-12-23" },
  "follow_up_gap_count": 533,
  "production_ranking": { "candidate": "b" }
}

9.Evaluation

Candidate A / B / C comparison

CandidateSort order
AHighest recorded Enforcement Action first → citations → recency
B (adopted)Citations first → highest recorded Enforcement Action → recency
CVisits with a citation first → highest recorded Enforcement Action → recency

Why Candidate B was selected

A top-20, staleness-focused review found Candidate A let a single old, severe enforcement action dominate the ranking regardless of recency: 5 of its top 20 centres had no new citation in 10+ months (its top 2 had none in ~16 months), versus 1 of 20 for Candidate B and 0 of 20 for Candidate C. Candidate B directly operationalizes "repeated non-compliance" — the criterion named first in Story #1 — as its primary key, while still using the recorded Enforcement Action order as a real secondary factor.

Inspection-volume bias

Across all 2,913 centres, total_inspection_visits correlates with total_non_compliance_citations at ≈0.73, and with visits_with_non_compliance at ≈0.79. Candidate C is not less volume-biased than B — it is slightly more — so switching to C would not have reduced this bias.

Same-visit citation inflation

A single inspection visit that produces many citations at once counts the same as many separate bad visits under Candidate B. One centre's 23 citations came from only 2 visits (a citations-per-bad-visit ratio of 11.5, versus a dataset median of 1.5); about 14% of actionable centres show a ratio of 3 or higher.

Staleness finding

Checked directly against the top 20 of each candidate for a most-recent-citation date more than ~10 months old: Candidate A — 5/20 stale; Candidate B — 1/20; Candidate C — 0/20. This was the decisive evidence against adopting Candidate A.

Dashboard / pipeline agreement

The production Priority Follow-Up List in dashboard/index.html reads centre-summary.json, which is generated and pre-sorted by scripts/build_centre_summary.py using the same Candidate B logic documented in CLAUDE.md and capability-map.md — the dashboard applies no independent client-side sort, so the two cannot diverge.

Final repository audit

Audit sectionResult
ConsistencyPASS
Capability alignmentPASS
GovernancePASS
DashboardPASS
Repository hygienePASS

"This project is ready for final submission with no blocking issues." — eval/final-project-audit.md.

10.Governance and Safety

Must Never Produce (CLAUDE.md)

  • No prediction — no field or model forecasts future inspections, violations, or outcomes.
  • No risk score — a combined "risk score" panel was explicitly considered and rejected (dashboard/dashboard-blueprint.md).
  • No invented severity field — the project applies a documented regulatory-action order to the existing Enforcement Action categories. This is a governed sorting rule, not an explicit severity field supplied by the dataset.
  • No resolved/closed claims — no such status field exists in the source data; only Remedy Date presence/absence can be stated.
  • Grounded explanations only — Capability 7's template uses only the aggregated facts already produced by Capabilities 3–4, nothing external or invented.
  • Final decisions remain with Priya — every ranking, gap, and explanation is a triage aid; the workspace does not decide what she should do about it.

11.Limitations

  • Inspection-volume bias — the ranking correlates with how often a centre has been inspected, not independently with how serious its situation is.
  • Same-visit citation inflation — one visit producing many citations at once counts the same as many separate bad visits.
  • Recency only as a tiebreaker — none of the three ranking candidates treat "how long ago" as a primary factor; it only breaks ties between otherwise-equal centres.
  • Missing fields — no inspector identity, no case-status (open/closed) field, no enrollment/attendance data, and no unique visit identifier exist in the source data.
  • Observed follow-up gaps do not prove unresolved cases — a missing later inspection row is reported as an observed absence in the record only, never as a claim that an issue remains open or unresolved.

12.Lessons Learned

  • Decision-first design — starting from Priya's exact recurring decision, rather than from what the data could technically support, kept the capability map to 8 items and the dashboard to 5 panels, with an explicit rejected-panels list (inspection-reason-mix view, a capacity chart, a combined risk score) rather than feature creep.
  • Deterministic first, language explanation second — building the ranking (Capability 4) as a fully auditable, reproducible sort before adding any natural-language layer (Capability 7) made the ranking itself easy to defend during governance review, and let the explanation layer stay strictly grounded in already-computed facts.
  • Governance before production lock-in — computing three candidate rankings and running a dedicated top-20/staleness review before picking one caught a real problem (Candidate A's stale-history domination) that a top-10-only, language-emphasis reading of Story #1 had missed.
  • Importance of evaluation and audit — a full repository consistency audit surfaced real, if minor, documentation drift (stale wording, an outdated closing sentence, a form-label mismatch for Capability 7) that would otherwise have gone unnoticed; fixing it before submission kept the documentation and the shipped dashboard in agreement.

13.Distinctness Statement

This is an individual project built entirely on the Alberta child care licensing inspection open dataset (data/raw/child-care-open-data-202512.csv). It is a distinct piece of work from the group Calgary 311 backlog-triage project completed earlier in this course.

AspectThis project (individual)Calgary 311 backlog-triage (group)
DatasetAlberta Children's Services child care licensing/inspection open dataCalgary's 311 municipal service-request data — a separate civic dataset
DomainProvincial child care regulatory complianceMunicipal 311 service-request operations
UserPriya Desai, Regional Program Manager, Licensing & Monitoring — a provincial regulatory inspection roleAn operations role responsible for triaging that project's own 311 request backlog
DecisionWhich licensed child care centres inspectors should follow up on firstWhich service requests in that project's backlog should be prioritized first
Operational contextProvincial licensing inspection and enforcement follow-upCity service-request fulfillment and backlog management

The two projects share a documentation methodology — business discovery, a capability map, a dashboard blueprint, and a governance review — adopted consistently across this student's coursework. That shared structure is a methodology choice, not shared data, users, or findings: no content, numbers, or conclusions in this report originate from the Calgary 311 project, and none of that project's specifics are asserted here beyond acknowledging it as a separate, earlier group deliverable in the same course.

Final production-ranking rule

  1. total_non_compliance_citations descending
  2. highest recorded Enforcement Action, by the recorded enforcement action order, descending
  3. most_recent_inspection_date descending — final tiebreaker only
